title: Harira
subtitle: Moroccan lamb, chickpea, and lentil soup
excerpt: Harira is the great Moroccan soup: lamb mince simmered with chickpeas, lentils, tomato, and a dense blend of warming spices, finished with a handful of fresh herbs and a squeeze of lemon. It is the soup eaten to break the fast during Ramadan, and good enough to eat on any evening.
sourceNotes:
• Paula Wolfert, *Couscous and Other Good Food from Morocco* (1973). Principal reference for the spice blend, the flour-thickening technique, and the herb finish.
• Claudia Roden, *The Food of Morocco* (2008). Cross-reference for the lamb-to-pulse ratio and the lemon-juice finishing note.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool fully and freeze for up to 3 months. The soup thickens on freezing — defrost overnight in the fridge and reheat on the hob with a splash of water.
recipe.batchNotes: Harira is designed to feed a crowd. The recipe makes 6 generous portions; double it easily in a large pot.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The soup improves overnight. Make the day before and reheat gently on the hob.

glossaryTerms.definition: The national soup of Morocco: a lamb, chickpea, and lentil broth spiced with ginger, cinnamon, and turmeric, thickened slightly with flour, and finished with fresh coriander, parsley, and lemon. Traditionally served to break the Ramadan fast.

content.text: is a soup built in stages. The lamb and spices go in first with the tomatoes and a little water, simmering until the meat is cooked through and the spices have bloomed. The pulses and stock follow, extending the soup into something substantial. The final step is the terdira: a thin flour-and-water paste stirred in at the end that gives the broth its characteristic slight body, somewhere between thin and thick.

The fresh herb finish is not optional. Coriander and flat-leaf parsley go in just before serving, and a squeeze of lemon at the table sharpens the whole soup. Without those finishing elements, harira is a good spiced broth; with them, it is a complete dish.

items.prepNote: mixed with 4 tbsp cold water to a smooth paste

content.text: A large, heavy pot gives enough room for the soup to expand with stock. Harira is a substantial soup that serves 6 generously.

Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a large, heavy pot over a medium-high heat. Add {{lamb-mince}} of lamb mince and cook, breaking it up with a spoon, for 5 to 6 minutes until browned. Add {{onion}} diced onion, {{celery}} celery sticks, and {{garlic}} of garlic. Cook for 5 minutes until softened.

Add {{ginger-root}} of grated ginger, {{turmeric}} of turmeric, {{cinnamon-ground}} of cinnamon, {{cumin-ground}} of ground cumin, and {{black-pepper}} of black pepper. Stir for 1 minute. Add {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes and 200 ml of water. Bring to a simmer and cook for 15 minutes.

Add {{chickpeas-tinned}} of drained chickpeas, {{lentils-brown}} of rinsed lentils, and {{stock-chicken}} of stock. Bring to a gentle simmer and cook for 30 minutes until the lentils are completely tender. Season generously with salt.

Stir the flour-and-water paste until smooth, then pour into the simmering soup while stirring constantly. Cook for 5 minutes, stirring frequently, until the soup thickens slightly. Remove from the heat and stir in half of the chopped coriander and parsley. Ladle into bowls, scatter the remaining herbs over each serving, and serve with lemon wedges for squeezing.

items.symptom: Flour paste has formed lumps in the soup
items.cause: The paste was added too quickly or not stirred continuously as it went in.
items.fix: Mix the flour paste in a separate bowl until completely smooth before adding. Pour it in a slow, steady stream while stirring the soup constantly. If lumps form, use a stick blender briefly to smooth them out.

items.cause: Too much flour paste was added, or the soup simmered too long after thickening.
items.fix: Stir in hot water or stock until the soup reaches the right consistency. Harira should be a hearty broth, not a stew — it should pour freely.

items.symptom: Lentils are still firm after 30 minutes
items.cause: The simmer was too gentle, or the lentils were old.
items.fix: Brown lentils take longer than red lentils — up to 40 minutes at a steady simmer. Check the packet date: old pulses take significantly longer to soften.

content.text: Harira is made across Morocco, Algeria, and Tunisia with regional variations in spicing and protein. The Moroccan version is the most codified, with the terdira thickening technique and the herb-and-lemon finish considered essential. It is the soup of Ramadan — prepared in large quantities and served at iftar to break the fast, often alongside dates, chebakia (honey-sesame pastries), and hard-boiled eggs. In Britain it arrived through Moroccan restaurant cooking from the 1980s onwards and is now well established in the repertoire of anyone who cooks North African food at home.
